title: Brown stew chicken
subtitle: Jamaican caramelised-sugar chicken braise with thyme and allspice
excerpt: Chicken pieces browned in burnt sugar to give a deep mahogany colour, then braised with thyme, allspice, onion, and scotch bonnet until the gravy is thick, dark, and fragrant. A Jamaican home kitchen staple.
sourceNotes: Brown stew chicken is a cornerstone of Jamaican home cooking, distinct from jerk (which is marinated and grilled) in its use of browning, a caramelisation technique using granulated or brown sugar in hot oil to produce a deep, slightly bitter colour on the chicken before braising. The technique of 'browning' in hot sugar is a defining characteristic of Jamaican stew dishes. This recipe represents the home-cook standard: browned chicken, a sofrito-style base of onion, spring onion, garlic, and thyme, and a scotch bonnet added whole.

content.text: Brown stew chicken gets its colour from a step that's rare in European cooking. You melt sugar in hot oil until it turns dark amber and bitter. Then you coat the chicken in it before any liquid goes in. The sugar doesn't taste sweet in the finished dish. It adds a deep, slightly burnt note that carries the thyme and allspice. The step is called 'browning' in Jamaican cooking. It applies to chicken, beef, and pork stews alike.

Marinate the chicken with the dry spices before you begin. This marinade is simpler than jerk, just salt, pepper, allspice, and curry powder for depth. Brown the chicken in batches so each piece gets proper contact with the hot caramel. The braising liquid is minimal; this is a stew, not a soup.

Toss the chicken pieces with {{allspice}} of allspice, {{curry-powder}} of curry powder, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper-ground}} of black pepper. Leave for at least 30 minutes at room temperature.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide, heavy pot or Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Sprinkle the {{dark-brown-sugar}} of sugar across the oil and let it melt and darken. It will turn from amber to a deep mahogany brown in about 2 minutes. Watch it carefully; burnt sugar past dark brown turns acrid. Working quickly, add the chicken pieces in a single layer and turn to coat each piece in the dark caramel. Cook for 3 minutes per side until the chicken has a deep, dark colour. Remove to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} onion rings, {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme sprigs, and {{tomato}} tomatoes. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring to combine with the residue in the pot. Return the chicken to the pot. Add 300 ml of water and the whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Bring to a simmer, then cover and cook over low heat for 40 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the gravy has thickened. If using ketchup, stir it in for the last 10 minutes. Remove the scotch bonnet and thyme before serving.

items.fix: Discard and start again with fresh oil and sugar over medium rather than high heat. Stay at the pan; the window from amber to burnt is about 30 seconds
items.cause: Heat too high or sugar left unattended
items.symptom: Sugar burning black and acrid before the chicken goes in

items.fix: Brown in two batches and wait until the sugar reaches a deep, nearly black amber before adding the chicken
items.cause: Pan overcrowded; chicken added before the sugar was dark enough

items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 15 minutes to concentrate the gravy. It should coat the chicken generously rather than pooling like soup
items.cause: Too much water added; lid not on during braising

items.fix: The sugar needs to be dark but not black. If the gravy tastes acrid, a teaspoon of ketchup and a squeeze of lime will help balance it
items.cause: Sugar taken past the point of caramel into burnt

content.text: Brown stew chicken is everyday food in the Jamaican home kitchen, appearing on weeknight dinner tables as often as it does at Sunday gatherings. The browning technique is so embedded in Jamaican cooking that it functions almost as a default method for stew-based dishes, whether chicken, beef, or pork. The finished dish is served with white rice or rice and peas. The gravy, which should be thick enough to coat the chicken without running, is the point of the dish; the rice is there to receive it.
